source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Figure shows the Diesel cycle. It is similar to the Otto cycle (see Problem CP19.71), but there are two important differences. First, the fuel is not admitted until the air is fully compressed at point 2. Because of the high temperature at the end of an adiabatic compression, the fuel begins to burn spontaneously. (There are no spark plugs in a diesel engine!) Second, combustion takes place more slowly, with fuel continuing to be injected. This makes the ignition stage a constant-pressure process. The cycle shown, for one cylinder of a diesel engine, has a displacement $V_{\text{max}} - V_{ ext{min}}$ of $1000 \, \text{cm}^3$ and a compression ratio $r = V_{\text{max}} / V_{\text{min}} = 21$. These are typical values for a diesel truck. The engine operates with intake air ($\gamma = 1.40$) at $25 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $1.0 \, \text{atm}$ pressure. The quantity of fuel injected into the cylinder has a heat of combustion of $1000 \, \text{J}$. What is the power output in $\text{kW}$ and horsepower ($1 \, \text{hp} = 746 \, \text{W}$) of an eight-cylinder diesel engine running at $2400 \, \text{rpm}$?

image_dependency: question explicitly references a figure: 'Figure shows the Diesel cycle' and 'The cycle shown'
requires_image=true and text_dominant=false in the sample fields
the task concerns a thermodynamic cycle diagram, which commonly encodes process ordering/state relations visually
registry_passed=true and one image asset is present, so the visual dependency is supported by the registry
multi_step: solving requires combining multiple given quantities: displacement, compression ratio, intake conditions, heat of combustion, cylinder count, and rpm
the problem involves several physics steps typical of a Diesel-cycle computation: state changes, cycle work, then engine power conversion
the prompt asks for power output in both kW and horsepower, adding an additional conversion step
question_text is long and procedurally dense, indicating nontrivial chained reasoning rather than single-step lookup
verifiability: answer is a concise numeric result ('211 kW'), which is checkable against calculation or the provided multiple-choice options
choices are provided and answer_text matches one choice exactly (D: 211 kW)
answer_type=short_text and registry_passed=true support straightforward downstream checking
verifiability is slightly reduced because the prompt asks for both kW and horsepower, while answer_text only includes the kW value

Initial scoring is strong overall (high image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability), indicating good candidate value for the pool.

sentence_segments.text: Figure shows the Diesel cycle.
It is similar to the Otto cycle (see Problem CP19.71), but there are two important differences.
First, the fuel is not admitted until the air is fully compressed at point 2.
Because of the high temperature at the end of an adiabatic compression, the fuel begins to burn spontaneously.
(There are no spark plugs in a diesel engine!) Second, combustion takes place more slowly, with fuel continuing to be injected.
This makes the ignition stage a constant-pressure process.
The cycle shown, for one cylinder of a diesel engine, has a displacement $V_{\text{max}} - V_{ ext{min}}$ of $1000 \, \text{cm}^3$ and a compression ratio $r = V_{\text{max}} / V_{\text{min}} = 21$.
These are typical values for a diesel truck.
The engine operates with intake air ($\gamma = 1.40$) at $25 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $1.0 \, \text{atm}$ pressure.
The quantity of fuel injected into the cylinder has a heat of combustion of $1000 \, \text{J}$.
What is the power output in $\text{kW}$ and horsepower ($1 \, \text{hp} = 746 \, \text{W}$) of an eight-cylinder diesel engine running at $2400 \, \text{rpm}$?

First, the fuel is not admitted until the air is fully compressed at point 2. Because of the high temperature at the end of an adiabatic compression, the fuel begins to burn spontaneously. (There are no spark plugs in a diesel engine!) Second, combustion takes place more slowly, with fuel continuing to be injected. This makes the ignition stage a constant-pressure process. The cycle shown, for one cylinder of a diesel engine, has a displacement $V_{\text{max}} - V_{\text{min}}$ of $1000 \, \text{cm}^3$ and a compression ratio $r = V_{\text{max}} / V_{\text{min}} = 21$. These are typical values for a diesel truck. The engine operates with intake air ($\gamma = 1.40$) at $25 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $1.0 \, \text{atm}$ pressure. The quantity of fuel injected into the cylinder has a heat of combustion of $1000 \, \text{J}$. What is the power output of the eight-cylinder diesel engine running at $2400 \, \text{rpm}$? Give your answer in $\text{kW}$.

open_ended_problem_variants.rewritten_question_text: Figure shows the Diesel cycle. It is similar to the Otto cycle (see Problem CP19.71), but there are two important differences. First, the fuel is not admitted until the air is fully compressed at point 2. Because of the high temperature at the end of an adiabatic compression, the fuel begins to burn spontaneously. (There are no spark plugs in a diesel engine!) Second, combustion takes place more slowly, with fuel continuing to be injected. This makes the ignition stage a constant-pressure process. The cycle shown, for one cylinder of a diesel engine, has a displacement $V_{\text{max}} - V_{\text{min}}$ of $1000 \, \text{cm}^3$ and a compression ratio $r = V_{\text{max}} / V_{\text{min}} = 21$. These are typical values for a diesel truck. The engine operates with intake air ($\gamma = 1.40$) at $25 \, ^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $1.0 \, \text{atm}$ pressure. The quantity of fuel injected into the cylinder has a heat of combustion of $1000 \, \text{J}$. What is the power output of the eight-cylinder diesel engine running at $2400 \, \text{rpm}$? Give your answer in $\text{kW}$.
